note: Adding error_threshold param to Image.quantize() (around line 1051-1116); will add MSE loop logic calling self.im.quantize repeatedly with increasing colors until error threshold met or colors cap reached. Also editing Tests/test_image_quantize.py to add tests.

type: SURVEY

type: CLAIM
files: src/PIL/Image.py,Tests/test_image_quantize.py
note: I'll own both files and implement both features (sort_palette + your error_threshold) since they touch the same quantize() method body. Please send a SPEC with exact signature, param name/default, behavior, loop/return semantics, and edge cases for error_threshold so I can implement it correctly.

type: CLAIM
files: src/PIL/Image.py
note: I will own src/PIL/Image.py and implement both error_threshold (mine) and sort_palette (yours) in quantize(). Please send SPEC for sort_palette so I can implement it correctly.

type: DEFER
files: Tests/test_image_quantize.py
spec: For error_threshold feature: quantize() gains new keyword-only-ish parameter 'error_threshold=0' (default 0 means disabled, preserving current behavior exactly). Signature becomes: quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, error_threshold=0). Behavior: when error_threshold > 0 and palette is None, after computing the quantized image, compute MSE between original image (converted to RGB if needed) and the quantized image (converted back to RGB via its palette). If MSE <= error_threshold, return that result immediately. If MSE > error_threshold, retry quantization with more colors (e.g. double colors each iteration, capped at 256) and repeat the MSE check, stopping and returning the best result once colors reaches 256 (the max) even if threshold still not met. When error_threshold == 0 (default), skip all of this new logic entirely -- behavior must be byte-identical to current quantize(). Suggested tests to add in Tests/test_image_quantize.py: (1) test that quantize(colors=..., error_threshold=X) returns an image whose color count is <= colors; (2) test that a low/lenient error_threshold (e.g. very high number like 1000000) results in fewer or equal colors compared to no threshold; (3) test that error_threshold=0 (default) produces identical output to calling quantize() without the parameter at all; (4) test with a complex/gradient image that a strict/low error_threshold causes it to use the max colors allowed. Let me know if you want a different parameter name or default.
note: error_threshold spec for test authoring

type: DEFER
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Add a new parameter 'sort_palette=False' to quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, sort_palette=False). Place it as the last positional/keyword param after dither for backward compatibility. Behavior: only applies to the non-reference-palette code path (i.e. after 'im = self._new(self.im.quantize(colors, method, kmeans))' and after im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)) is set — the palette= branch that returns early should NOT be affected). When sort_palette is truthy, after building im.palette, do the following: 1) Extract the used palette entries as RGB tuples: palette_bytes = im.im.getpalette('RGB', 'RGB'); build a list of (r,g,b) tuples for each of the palette entries actually used (0..colors-1, or better, only indices present in im.im via im.im.getcolors() / actual color count from the quantize result — the palette array may be longer than the number of colors actually used, so only reorder entries that are in-use to avoid touching padding). 2) Compute a sort key per palette index: if sort_palette == 'luminance' or True (default true behavior), sort by perceptual brightness: key = 0.299*r + 0.587*g + 0.114*b (standard luma formula). If sort_palette == 'rgb', sort by the raw (r, g, b) tuple. Accept sort_palette as either a bool (True => luminance sort, False => no sorting, preserve current behavior/order) or a string: 'luminance' (default when True) or 'rgb'. 3) Determine the new order of palette indices (old_index -> new_index) via sorted(range(n), key=...). 4) Remap the pixel data: build a lookup/translation table old_index -> new_position (size 256, identity for unused/untouched indices) and call im.im.point(mapping_table, 'L') or use im.remap_palette(new_positions) if such a helper exists (check ImagePalette/core for an existing remap mechanism used elsewhere, e.g. Image.remap_palette is already a public method on Image — reuse Image.remap_palette(dest_map) if it fits, otherwise implement equivalent logic inline). 5) Reorder im.palette's palette bytes to match the new order so palette entry i corresponds to the pixel index i after remapping. Edge cases: if sort_palette is False/falsy (default), do nothing — output must be byte-for-byte identical to current behavior (this preserves backward compatibility, critical since it's the default). If colors used < colors requested, only sort/remap the entries that are actually present. Must not raise for palette images with fewer than requested colors. Must work for mode 'P' output only (this is the quantize() output mode already). No dependency on numpy or other new libraries. Tests I will write against this in Tests/test_image_quantize.py will call im.quantize(sort_palette=True) and im.quantize(sort_palette='rgb') and assert result.mode == 'P', and check that palette getcolor entries are non-decreasing in luminance for the default/luminance case (allow using ImageStat or manual brightness calc on im.getpalette() entries in order of the colors actually used, e.g. via im.convert('RGB').getcolors() correlated with palette, or simpler: check that the sequence of palette RGB triples used in the image's histogram order is sorted). Also test that sort_palette=False (default) leaves output unchanged from current quantize() behavior (compare to a call without sort_palette at all — should be pixel-identical).
note: This is my full feature spec; let me know if anything is ambiguous. I'll own Tests/test_image_quantize.py and write tests against this contract, plus tests for your error_threshold feature if you send me its spec too.

type: DEFER
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Same spec as already sent for Tests/test_image_quantize.py: add parameter error_threshold=0 to quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, error_threshold=0). When error_threshold<=0 (default), behavior must be byte-identical to current implementation (skip all new logic). When error_threshold>0 and palette is None: after quantizing at the requested colors count, compute MSE between original image (as RGB) and the quantized result converted back to RGB. If MSE <= error_threshold, return as-is. Otherwise increase colors (e.g. double, cap at 256) and re-quantize, repeating until MSE<=error_threshold or colors reaches 256 (then return the 256-color result regardless of MSE). If palette is provided (reference palette path), error_threshold should be ignored (that code path returns early as today). Raise no new exceptions; error_threshold is purely additive.
note: Confirming: you own both src/PIL/Image.py and Tests/test_image_quantize.py and will implement error_threshold (per spec) alongside your sort_palette feature. I will not edit either file.

type: CLAIM
files: src/PIL/Image.py,Tests/test_image_quantize.py
note: Acknowledged. I own both files and will implement error_threshold=0 per your spec (byte-identical when 0, MSE-based re-quantize loop capped at 256 colors otherwise, ignored when palette is provided) alongside my sort_palette feature. Will send DONE when finished.

type: CLAIM
files: src/PIL/Image.py
note: IMPORTANT correction: per my last CLAIM message (which may have crossed with yours), I own src/PIL/Image.py and have ALREADY implemented + tested BOTH error_threshold and sort_palette there. Please do NOT edit Image.py -- only edit Tests/test_image_quantize.py. Final contract for your tests: quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, error_threshold=0, sort_palette=False). error_threshold: when >0, starts at n=min(2,colors) colors, doubles n (capped at 'colors', NOT 256) each round, computing MSE (via new module-level _quantize_mse helper: mean of squared RGB-channel RMS, i.e. ImageChops.difference on RGB-converted images + ImageStat.Stat.rms) between original and candidate; stops and returns as soon as MSE<=error_threshold or n>=colors. When error_threshold==0 (default) or palette is given, behavior is byte-identical to unmodified quantize(). sort_palette: False (default, no-op, byte-identical output) | True (sort used palette entries by luminance 0.299r+0.587g+0.114b) | 'rgb' (sort by raw (r,g,b) tuple); implemented via new module-level _sort_quantized_palette(im, sort_palette) which uses im.histogram() to find used indices and im.remap_palette(dest_map) to reorder+remap. Both already verified working via manual script and full existing test suite (Tests/test_image_quantize.py) still passes unmodified.

type: DONE
files: src/PIL/Image.py,Tests/test_image_quantize.py
note: Implemented sort_palette (True/'luminance'/'rgb') and error_threshold (per your spec: MSE-based doubling loop capped at 256 colors, skipped when 0 or when palette= is given) in quantize(). Added tests for both. All existing + new tests pass (13 passed in test_image_quantize.py). Reused Image.remap_palette() for the palette reordering.
